title: Network Scanning & Reconnaissance with Python
description: Build your own network scanning tools — port scanners, service fingerprinters, banner grabbers, and reconnaissance scripts using Python sockets, scapy, and nmap libraries.
learning_outcomes: Build a TCP connect scanner from scratch using Python sockets
Implement SYN stealth scanning with Scapy
Perform service fingerprinting and banner grabbing
Enumerate open ports and identify running services
Use python-nmap for automated network discovery
Implement ICMP ping sweeps to find live hosts
Build DNS enumeration and reverse lookup tools
Detect operating systems through TCP/IP fingerprinting
Understand legal and ethical boundaries of scanning
Apply scanning techniques in authorised penetration testing
Parse and analyse scan results programmatically
Build a complete reconnaissance toolkit in Python

explanation_styles.analogy: Network scanning is like being a security guard checking every door in a skyscraper. Port 80 is the main entrance (web), port 22 is the staff door (SSH), port 443 is the VIP entrance (HTTPS). A port scanner systematically tries every door to see which ones open.
explanation_styles.visual: Imagine a grid: rows are IP addresses, columns are port numbers. Green cells = open, Red cells = closed, Grey cells = filtered. Your scanner fills in this grid one cell at a time, building a map of the network.
explanation_styles.step_by_step: 1. Create a socket (your 'hand' that will knock on doors), 2. Set a timeout (don't wait forever at each door), 3. Try to connect to IP:port (knock), 4. If it connects = OPEN (door opened!), 5. If refused = CLOSED (door locked), 6. If timeout = FILTERED (no answer, maybe firewall).
explanation_styles.code_trace: Let's trace socket.connect_ex(('target', 80)): The OS sends a TCP SYN packet to port 80. If the service replies with SYN-ACK, connect_ex returns 0 (success = port open). If it replies RST, returns errno (port closed). If nothing comes back within timeout, returns ETIMEDOUT (filtered).
explanation_styles.comparison: TCP Connect scan = knocking on the door and walking in (easy but noisy). SYN scan = knocking and running away before the door fully opens (stealthy). UDP scan = sliding a note under the door and waiting for a response (slow, unreliable). Each has trade-offs in speed, stealth, and reliability.

theory.introduction: Welcome to the world of network reconnaissance — the art and science of discovering what's running on a network before you test its security. Every penetration test, every security audit, every bug bounty starts the same way: scanning. Think of a network like a massive apartment building with 65,535 doors (ports) on every floor (IP address). Some doors are wide open with services running behind them — web servers, databases, SSH terminals. Some are locked. Some have security guards (firewalls) standing in front. Your job as a security professional is to systematically check every door, identify what's behind the open ones, and document everything you find. In this module, you'll build your own scanning tools from scratch using Python — the same language used by professional penetration testers worldwide. You'll start with simple TCP connect scans, graduate to stealthy SYN scans with Scapy, learn banner grabbing and service fingerprinting, and finish by building a complete reconnaissance toolkit. Every exercise either runs in the platform's code editor or on a Kali Linux VM for hands-on network work. By the end, you'll understand not just HOW to scan, but WHY each technique works at the packet level — knowledge that separates script kiddies from real security professionals.
theory.why_important: Network scanning is the foundation of ALL security testing. Without knowing what ports are open, what services are running, and what versions are installed, you cannot find vulnerabilities. The Equifax breach (2017, 147 million records stolen) happened because an unpatched Apache Struts server was running on a port that nobody was monitoring. Regular scanning would have found the vulnerable service. Every compliance framework (PCI DSS, ISO 27001, NIST) requires regular vulnerability scanning. As a security professional, scanning is your most-used skill — and understanding how scanners work at the code level gives you a massive advantage over people who just click buttons in tools.

key_concepts.explanation: Every networked computer has 65,535 TCP ports and 65,535 UDP ports — think of them as numbered doors in a building. Well-known ports (0-1023) are like labelled doors: port 80 says 'Web Server', port 22 says 'SSH', port 443 says 'Secure Web'. Registered ports (1024-49151) are application-specific, and dynamic ports (49152-65535) are temporary. When you scan, you're checking which doors are open, closed, or filtered by a firewall.
key_concepts.example:
import socket

# Check if port 80 is open on a target
sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
sock.settimeout(2)
result = sock.connect_ex(('scanme.nmap.org', 80))
if result == 0:
    print('Port 80 is OPEN')
else:
    print(f'Port 80 is closed/filtered (error: {result})')
sock.close()
key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of ports like TV channels — each number has a different show (service) playing. Channel 80 = web browsing, Channel 22 = remote terminal, Channel 25 = email delivery.

key_concepts.explanation: Before any TCP communication happens, two computers perform a 'handshake' — like shaking hands before a meeting. The client sends SYN ('Hello, can we talk?'), the server replies SYN-ACK ('Sure, I'm ready!'), and the client confirms with ACK ('Great, let's go!'). This is crucial for scanning because: if you get SYN-ACK back, the port is OPEN. If you get RST (Reset), the port is CLOSED. If you get nothing, the port is FILTERED (firewall blocking it). Understanding this handshake is the key to understanding every scan type.
key_concepts.example:
# The TCP handshake in action (conceptual):
# Step 1: Client -> SYN -> Server  ('Can we talk?')
# Step 2: Server -> SYN-ACK -> Client  ('Yes, I'm here!')
# Step 3: Client -> ACK -> Server  ('Great, connected!')

# In Python, socket.connect() does all 3 steps.
# socket.connect_ex() returns 0 if all 3 succeed (port open)
# Returns an error code if step 2 fails (port closed/filtered)

import socket
sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
sock.settimeout(1)
result = sock.connect_ex(('example.com', 443))
print(f'Handshake result: {result}')  # 0 = success
sock.close()
key_concepts.ai_hint: It's like calling someone on the phone: you dial (SYN), they say 'Hello?' (SYN-ACK), you say 'Hi, it's me' (ACK). If the line is dead = closed. If it just rings forever = filtered.

key_concepts.explanation: The simplest and most reliable scan type. Your scanner completes the full TCP handshake with each port — if the handshake succeeds, the port is open. It's like actually walking through every door in the building. The advantage: it always works and doesn't need special permissions. The disadvantage: it's noisy — the target's logs will record every connection. In Python, this is just socket.connect() or socket.connect_ex() in a loop over ports.
key_concepts.example:
import socket

def tcp_connect_scan(target, ports):
    open_ports = []
    for port in ports:
        sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        sock.settimeout(1)
        result = sock.connect_ex((target, port))
        if result == 0:
            open_ports.append(port)
            print(f'  [OPEN] Port {port}')
        sock.close()
    return open_ports

# Scan common ports
print('Scanning scanme.nmap.org...')
found = tcp_connect_scan('scanme.nmap.org', [22, 80, 443, 8080])
print(f'
Open ports: {found}')
key_concepts.ai_hint: TCP Connect = the polite visitor who actually enters through every open door and says hello. Reliable but everyone knows you were there!

key_concepts.explanation: The most popular scan type in professional pen testing. Instead of completing the full handshake, you only send SYN and wait for SYN-ACK (port open) or RST (port closed). You never send the final ACK — so the connection is never fully established. It's like knocking on a door and running away before it opens fully. Benefits: faster (no full handshake overhead) and stealthier (many older systems don't log half-open connections). Requires raw socket access (root/admin privileges). Scapy makes this easy in Python.
key_concepts.example:
# SYN Scan with Scapy (requires root/sudo)
from scapy.all import *

def syn_scan(target, port):
    # Send SYN packet
    syn_pkt = IP(dst=target)/TCP(dport=port, flags='S')
    response = sr1(syn_pkt, timeout=2, verbose=0)
    
    if response is None:
        return 'filtered'
    elif response.haslayer(TCP):
        if response[TCP].flags == 0x12:  # SYN-ACK
            # Send RST to close (don't complete handshake)
            rst = IP(dst=target)/TCP(dport=port, flags='R')
            send(rst, verbose=0)
            return 'open'
        elif response[TCP].flags == 0x14:  # RST-ACK
            return 'closed'
    return 'filtered'

# Usage (run as root on Kali VM):
# result = syn_scan('target_ip', 80)
key_concepts.ai_hint: SYN scan = the ninja who knocks, peeks through the keyhole, and vanishes. You learn who's home without them knowing you visited!

key_concepts.explanation: Finding an open port is just the beginning — you need to know WHAT'S running behind it. Banner grabbing means connecting to an open port and reading the welcome message (banner) the service sends. For example, connecting to port 22 might reveal 'SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_8.9p1 Ubuntu'. This tells you the exact software version — and that's how you find vulnerabilities. Some services send banners automatically, others require you to send a specific request first (like HTTP GET for web servers).
key_concepts.example:
import socket

def grab_banner(target, port, timeout=3):
    try:
        sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        sock.settimeout(timeout)
        sock.connect((target, port))
        
        # Some services send banner immediately
        try:
            banner = sock.recv(1024).decode('utf-8', errors='replace').strip()
        except socket.timeout:
            # Others need a prompt — try HTTP
            sock.send(b'HEAD / HTTP/1.1
Host: ' + target.encode() + b'

')
            banner = sock.recv(4096).decode('utf-8', errors='replace').strip()
        
        sock.close()
        return banner
    except Exception as e:
        return f'Error: {e}'

# Example
print(grab_banner('scanme.nmap.org', 80))
key_concepts.ai_hint: Banner grabbing is like reading the nameplate on a door — it tells you who lives there. 'SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_8.9' = knowing the exact lock model so you can check for known weaknesses.

key_concepts.explanation: Before scanning ports, you need to find which hosts are actually alive on the network. A ping sweep sends ICMP Echo Request packets to every address in a range and listens for replies. It's like yelling 'Hello!' in every room of a building and noting which rooms answer back. Some hosts block ICMP (firewall rules), so professional scanners combine ICMP with TCP ping (SYN to common ports) and ARP requests on local networks.
key_concepts.ai_hint: Ping sweep is like a roll call — shout every name and see who answers 'Here!' Some people stay quiet on purpose (firewalls blocking ICMP).

key_concepts.explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) is a goldmine for reconnaissance. Forward lookups convert domain names to IP addresses, reverse lookups do the opposite. Subdomain enumeration finds hidden services (dev.company.com, staging.company.com, admin.company.com). Zone transfers, when misconfigured, can dump an organisation's entire DNS database. Python's socket.getaddrinfo() and the dnspython library make this easy.
key_concepts.ai_hint: DNS enumeration is like checking the building directory in the lobby — it lists every office (subdomain) and which floor (IP) they're on.

key_concepts.explanation: While TCP scanning gets all the attention, many critical services run on UDP: DNS (53), SNMP (161), DHCP (67/68), NTP (123), and TFTP (69). UDP scanning is harder because UDP is connectionless — there's no handshake. You send a packet and hope for a response. If you get an ICMP 'port unreachable' back, the port is closed. If you get nothing back, the port could be open OR filtered (firewall eating the packet). UDP scans are slow and unreliable but essential for thorough assessments.
key_concepts.ai_hint: UDP scanning is like posting letters without tracking — you send them out but can't be sure they arrived. Silence might mean 'received' or 'lost in the mail.'

key_concepts.explanation: Nmap is the world's most powerful port scanner. python-nmap is a Python wrapper that lets you control Nmap programmatically — perfect for building automated security tools. You get all of Nmap's scanning capabilities (SYN, version detection, OS fingerprinting, NSE scripts) with the convenience of Python data structures. The results come back as dictionaries you can parse, filter, and report on.
key_concepts.example:
import nmap

def automated_scan(target, ports='1-1000'):
    """Run an nmap scan and parse results."""
    scanner = nmap.PortScanner()
    
    print(f'Scanning {target}...')
    scanner.scan(target, ports, arguments='-sV -sC')
    
    for host in scanner.all_hosts():
        print(f'
Host: {host} ({scanner[host].hostname()})')
        print(f'State: {scanner[host].state()}')
        
        for proto in scanner[host].all_protocols():
            ports_list = scanner[host][proto].keys()
            for port in sorted(ports_list):
                info = scanner[host][proto][port]
                print(f'  {port}/{proto}  {info["state"]}  '
                      f'{info.get("name","")}  '
                      f'{info.get("product","")} '
                      f'{info.get("version","")}')

# Usage: automated_scan('scanme.nmap.org')
key_concepts.ai_hint: python-nmap is like having a robot that operates Nmap's control panel for you — same power, but you can script it into automated workflows!

key_concepts.explanation: Different operating systems implement TCP/IP slightly differently — the initial TTL value, TCP window size, IP ID sequence, and response to unusual packets all vary. By analysing these differences, scanners can guess the target's OS with high confidence. Nmap's OS detection (-O flag) sends a series of crafted packets and compares responses against a database of 6,000+ OS fingerprints. This is like identifying a car brand by the sound of its engine.
key_concepts.ai_hint: OS fingerprinting is like identifying someone by their walk — every OS has a unique 'digital walk' in how it handles network packets.

key_concepts.explanation: Security devices (IDS/IPS, firewalls) watch for scanning activity. Sending 10,000 SYN packets per second is obvious. Professional scanners use timing controls: slow scans (one port per minute) fly under the radar, randomised port order prevents pattern detection, and fragmented packets can bypass some firewalls. Nmap's timing templates (-T0 through -T5) range from paranoid to insane. Understanding evasion is crucial for both attackers (pen testers) and defenders (writing detection rules).
key_concepts.example:
import socket
import random
import time

def stealth_scan(target, ports, delay=0.5):
    """Slow scan with randomised port order."""
    shuffled = list(ports)
    random.shuffle(shuffled)  # Random order
    open_ports = []
    
    for port in shuffled:
        try:
            sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
            sock.settimeout(2)
            result = sock.connect_ex((target, port))
            if result == 0:
                open_ports.append(port)
            sock.close()
        except:
            pass
        time.sleep(delay)  # Wait between probes
    
    return sorted(open_ports)

# Nmap timing equivalents:
# -T0 (Paranoid): 5 min between probes
# -T1 (Sneaky): 15 sec between probes
# -T2 (Polite): 0.4 sec between probes
# -T3 (Normal): default parallel scanning
# -T4 (Aggressive): faster, less reliable
# -T5 (Insane): fastest, may miss ports
key_concepts.ai_hint: Scan timing is like burglary vs house inspection — running through the building triggers alarms; walking calmly room by room looks normal.

key_concepts.explanation: CRITICAL: Scanning without permission is illegal in most countries. The UK Computer Misuse Act 1990, the US CFAA, and the EU Directive on Attacks Against Information Systems all criminalise unauthorised scanning. Always get written permission (scope document) before scanning. Use only authorised targets: your own machines, scanme.nmap.org (Nmap's test server), HackTheBox/TryHackMe labs, and explicitly permitted client networks. Professional pen testers carry signed authorisation letters during engagements. Even accidental scanning of wrong IP ranges has resulted in criminal charges.
key_concepts.example:
# LEGAL SCANNING CHECKLIST:
# ✅ Your own machines on your own network
# ✅ scanme.nmap.org (Nmap's official test target)
# ✅ HackTheBox / TryHackMe lab machines
# ✅ Client networks WITH signed authorisation
# ✅ Cloud instances YOU own (AWS/Azure/GCP)
#
# ❌ NEVER scan without written permission
# ❌ NEVER scan your ISP's network
# ❌ NEVER scan random internet IPs

# ❌ NEVER scan government systems
#
# SAFE EXERCISE SETUP:
targets = {
    'legal': [
        'scanme.nmap.org',     # Nmap's test server

        '127.0.0.1',           # Loopback
    ],

}

key_concepts.ai_hint: Think of it like keys — you can test locks on YOUR house, locks you've been HIRED to test, and locks in a TRAINING facility. Testing random locks on the street = jail time.

complete_examples.title: Example 1: Simple Single-Port Check
complete_examples.description: The most basic scan — check if one specific port is open on a target.
complete_examples.code:
import socket

def check_port(host, port):
    """Check if a single port is open."""
    sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    sock.settimeout(3)
    
    result = sock.connect_ex((host, port))
    sock.close()
    
    if result == 0:
        return True   # Port is open
    else:
        return False  # Port is closed or filtered

# Test against Nmap's public test server
target = 'scanme.nmap.org'

print(f'Checking ports on {target}...')
print(f'Port 22 (SSH):   {"OPEN" if check_port(target, 22) else "CLOSED"}')
print(f'Port 80 (HTTP):  {"OPEN" if check_port(target, 80) else "CLOSED"}')
print(f'Port 443 (HTTPS): {"OPEN" if check_port(target, 443) else "CLOSED"}')
print(f'Port 8080:       {"OPEN" if check_port(target, 8080) else "CLOSED"}')
complete_examples.output:
Checking ports on scanme.nmap.org...
Port 22 (SSH):   OPEN
Port 80 (HTTP):  OPEN
Port 443 (HTTPS): CLOSED
Port 8080:       CLOSED
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Line by line:
1. socket.socket() creates a TCP connection object
2. settimeout(3) means 'wait max 3 seconds per port'
3. connect_ex() tries the TCP handshake — returns 0 if successful (port open)
4. We close the socket after each check to free resources
5. The function returns True/False for clean code
6. We test 4 common ports on Nmap's legal test server
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Modify this to also show the error code when a port is closed. What does error code 111 mean? What about 110?

complete_examples.title: Example 2: Multi-Port Scanner with Timing
complete_examples.description: Scan a range of ports and measure how long the scan takes.
complete_examples.code:
import socket
import time

def scan_ports(target, port_range):
    """Scan a range of ports, return list of open ones."""
    open_ports = []
    start_time = time.time()
    
    print(f'Scanning {target} ports {port_range[0]}-{port_range[-1]}...')
    print('-' * 40)
    
    for port in port_range:
        sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        sock.settimeout(1)
        
        result = sock.connect_ex((target, port))
        if result == 0:
            try:
                service = socket.getservbyport(port, 'tcp')
            except OSError:
                service = 'unknown'
            open_ports.append((port, service))
            print(f'  [OPEN] Port {port:5d} -> {service}')
        sock.close()
    
    elapsed = time.time() - start_time
    print('-' * 40)
    print(f'Scan complete: {len(open_ports)} open ports found')
    print(f'Time elapsed: {elapsed:.2f} seconds')
    print(f'Scan speed: {len(port_range)/elapsed:.0f} ports/second')
    
    return open_ports

# Scan first 100 ports
results = scan_ports('scanme.nmap.org', range(1, 101))
complete_examples.output:
Scanning scanme.nmap.org ports 1-100...
----------------------------------------
  [OPEN] Port    22 -> ssh
  [OPEN] Port    80 -> http
----------------------------------------
Scan complete: 2 open ports found
Time elapsed: 12.45 seconds
Scan speed: 8 ports/second
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Key improvements over Example 1:
1. Scans a RANGE of ports using a for loop
2. Uses socket.getservbyport() to look up the service name (ssh, http, etc.)
3. Measures elapsed time with time.time()
4. Calculates scan speed (ports per second)
5. timeout=1 keeps it fast — closed ports fail quickly
6. Note: sequential scanning is slow (~8 ports/sec). We'll fix that with threading later!

complete_examples.title: Example 3: Threaded Port Scanner (Fast!)
complete_examples.description: Use threading to scan hundreds of ports in seconds instead of minutes.
complete_examples.code:
import socket
import threading
import time
from queue import Queue

class ThreadedScanner:
    def __init__(self, target, threads=100):
        self.target = target
        self.threads = threads
        self.open_ports = []
        self.lock = threading.Lock()
        self.port_queue = Queue()
    
    def scan_port(self):
        """Worker thread: scan ports from the queue."""
        while not self.port_queue.empty():
            port = self.port_queue.get()
            try:
                sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
                sock.settimeout(1)
                result = sock.connect_ex((self.target, port))
                if result == 0:
                    with self.lock:
                        self.open_ports.append(port)
                sock.close()
            except:
                pass
            self.port_queue.task_done()
    
    def scan(self, start_port=1, end_port=1024):
        """Scan port range using thread pool."""
        # Fill the queue
        for port in range(start_port, end_port + 1):
            self.port_queue.put(port)
        
        start = time.time()
        print(f'Scanning {self.target} ({end_port - start_port + 1} ports, {self.threads} threads)...')
        
        # Launch threads
        thread_list = []
        for _ in range(self.threads):
            t = threading.Thread(target=self.scan_port, daemon=True)
            t.start()
            thread_list.append(t)
        
        # Wait for completion
        self.port_queue.join()
        
        elapsed = time.time() - start
        self.open_ports.sort()
        
        print(f'\nResults ({elapsed:.2f}s):')
        for port in self.open_ports:
            try:
                service = socket.getservbyport(port, 'tcp')
            except:
                service = 'unknown'
            print(f'  {port:5d}/tcp  OPEN  {service}')
        
        print(f'\n{len(self.open_ports)} open ports found')
        print(f'Speed: {(end_port - start_port + 1)/elapsed:.0f} ports/second')
        return self.open_ports

# Usage
scanner = ThreadedScanner('scanme.nmap.org', threads=50)
scanner.scan(1, 1024)
complete_examples.output:
Scanning scanme.nmap.org (1024 ports, 50 threads)...

Results (3.21s):
   22/tcp  OPEN  ssh
   80/tcp  OPEN  http

2 open ports found
Speed: 319 ports/second
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Massive speed improvement! Here's how threading works:
1. A Queue holds all ports to scan
2. 50 worker threads each grab a port, scan it, grab the next
3. A Lock prevents race conditions when adding to open_ports
4. daemon=True means threads die when the main program exits
5. port_queue.join() waits until all ports are processed
6. Result: 319 ports/sec vs 8 ports/sec — 40x faster!
7. The threading.Lock() is crucial — without it, two threads might write to the list simultaneously and corrupt data

complete_examples.title: Example 5: ICMP Ping Sweep with Scapy
complete_examples.description: Discover live hosts on a network using ICMP and ARP with Scapy.
complete_examples.code:
# This script requires Scapy and root privileges
# Run on Kali VM: sudo python3 ping_sweep.py

from scapy.all import *
import ipaddress

def icmp_sweep(network_cidr):
    """ICMP ping sweep to find live hosts."""
    network = ipaddress.ip_network(network_cidr, strict=False)
    live_hosts = []
    
    print(f'ICMP Sweep: {network_cidr}')
    print(f'Checking {network.num_addresses - 2} hosts...\n')
    
    for ip in network.hosts():
        pkt = IP(dst=str(ip))/ICMP()
        reply = sr1(pkt, timeout=1, verbose=0)
        
        if reply is not None:
            ttl = reply[IP].ttl
            # Guess OS from TTL
            if ttl <= 64:
                os_guess = 'Linux/Unix'
            elif ttl <= 128:
                os_guess = 'Windows'
            else:
                os_guess = 'Network device'
            
            live_hosts.append({
                'ip': str(ip),
                'ttl': ttl,
                'os_guess': os_guess
            })
            print(f'  [ALIVE] {str(ip):15s}  TTL={ttl:3d}  ({os_guess})')
    
    print(f'\n{len(live_hosts)} hosts alive out of {network.num_addresses - 2}')
    return live_hosts

def arp_sweep(network_cidr):
    """ARP sweep for local network (faster, more reliable)."""
    print(f'\nARP Sweep: {network_cidr}')
    answered, _ = srp(
        Ether(dst='ff:ff:ff:ff:ff:ff')/ARP(pdst=network_cidr),
        timeout=2, verbose=0
    )
    
    for sent, received in answered:
        print(f'  [ALIVE] {received[ARP].psrc:15s}  MAC: {received[Ether].src}')
    
    return len(answered)

# Usage (on Kali VM with target network):
# icmp_sweep('192.168.56.0/24')
# arp_sweep('192.168.56.0/24')
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Two discovery methods:
1. ICMP sweep: Send Echo Request to every IP, wait for replies
   - TTL in reply hints at OS (Linux~64, Windows~128)
   - Can be blocked by firewalls
2. ARP sweep: Ask 'who has this IP?' on local network
   - Works on local subnet only (same LAN)
   - Cannot be blocked by software firewalls!
   - Also reveals MAC addresses
3. sr1() = send-receive-1 (send one packet, wait for one reply)
4. srp() = send-receive at layer 2 (Ethernet level for ARP)
5. verbose=0 suppresses Scapy's default output
complete_examples.ai_challenge: Combine both methods: first ARP sweep (reliable on LAN), then ICMP sweep for any hosts ARP missed. Compare results and flag discrepancies.

complete_examples.title: Example 6: SYN Stealth Scan with Scapy
complete_examples.description: Build a SYN scanner that never completes the TCP handshake — professional pen testing technique.
complete_examples.code:
# SYN Stealth Scan — requires root privileges
# Run on Kali VM: sudo python3 syn_scan.py

from scapy.all import *
import random

def syn_scan(target, ports):
    """SYN stealth scan — half-open, never completes handshake."""
    results = {'open': [], 'closed': [], 'filtered': []}
    
    # Randomise port order for evasion
    scan_ports = list(ports)
    random.shuffle(scan_ports)
    
    print(f'SYN Scan: {target} ({len(scan_ports)} ports)')
    print(f'Source port: random | Flags: SYN only\n')
    
    for port in scan_ports:
        # Random source port for each probe
        src_port = random.randint(40000, 65000)
        
        # Craft SYN packet
        syn = IP(dst=target)/TCP(
            sport=src_port, dport=port,
            flags='S',  # SYN only
            seq=random.randint(0, 2**32)
        )
        
        # Send and wait for response
        response = sr1(syn, timeout=2, verbose=0)
        
        if response is None:
            results['filtered'].append(port)
        elif response.haslayer(TCP):
            flags = response[TCP].flags
            if flags == 0x12:  # SYN-ACK = open
                results['open'].append(port)
                # Send RST to close cleanly
                rst = IP(dst=target)/TCP(
                    sport=src_port, dport=port,
                    flags='R', seq=response[TCP].ack
                )
                send(rst, verbose=0)
            elif flags & 0x04:  # RST = closed
                results['closed'].append(port)
        elif response.haslayer(ICMP):
            results['filtered'].append(port)
    
    # Print results
    results['open'].sort()
    print('Open ports:')
    for p in results['open']:
        print(f'  {p}/tcp  OPEN')
    print(f'\nSummary: {len(results["open"])} open, '
          f'{len(results["closed"])} closed, '
          f'{len(results["filtered"])} filtered')
    
    return results

# Usage (root on Kali):
# syn_scan('target_ip', range(1, 1025))
complete_examples.output:
SYN Scan: 192.168.56.101 (1024 ports)
Source port: random | Flags: SYN only

Open ports:
  22/tcp  OPEN
  80/tcp  OPEN
  443/tcp OPEN

Summary: 3 open, 1019 closed, 2 filtered
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
Professional SYN scan breakdown:
1. flags='S' sends ONLY the SYN flag — first step of handshake
2. If target replies SYN-ACK (0x12) = port is open
3. We immediately send RST to tear down — handshake never completes
4. If target replies RST (0x04) = port is closed
5. If no reply = port is filtered (firewall ate the packet)
6. Random source port + shuffled order = harder to detect
7. This is exactly how nmap -sS works internally
8. Requires root because raw sockets need elevated privileges

complete_examples.code:
import socket
import concurrent.futures

def dns_resolve(domain):
    """Resolve a domain to its IP addresses."""
    try:
        results = socket.getaddrinfo(domain, None, socket.AF_INET)
        ips = list(set(r[4][0] for r in results))
        return ips
    except socket.gaierror:
        return []

def reverse_dns(ip):
    """Reverse lookup: IP to hostname."""
    try:
        return socket.gethostbyaddr(ip)[0]
    except:
        return None

def subdomain_enum(domain, wordlist):
    """Brute-force subdomain enumeration."""
    found = []
    
    def check_sub(sub):
        full = f'{sub}.{domain}'
        ips = dns_resolve(full)
        if ips:
            return (full, ips)
        return None
    
    print(f'Enumerating subdomains of {domain}...')
    print(f'Wordlist: {len(wordlist)} entries\n')
    
    with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=20) as ex:
        futures = {ex.submit(check_sub, s): s for s in wordlist}
        for future in concurrent.futures.as_completed(futures):
            result = future.result()
            if result:
                found.append(result)
                print(f'  [FOUND] {result[0]:30s} -> {result[1]}')
    
    print(f'\n{len(found)} subdomains found')
    return found

# Common subdomain wordlist
wordlist = [
    'www', 'mail', 'ftp', 'admin', 'dev', 'staging',
    'api', 'test', 'blog', 'shop', 'cdn', 'static',
    'portal', 'vpn', 'remote', 'db', 'mysql', 'postgres',
    'git', 'gitlab', 'jenkins', 'ci', 'internal', 'intranet',
    'backup', 'monitor', 'grafana', 'kibana', 'elastic',
    'docs', 'wiki', 'help', 'support', 'status'
]

# Example usage
subdomain_enum('nmap.org', wordlist)

import nmap

def comprehensive_scan(target):
    """Run a full service detection scan using python-nmap."""
    scanner = nmap.PortScanner()
    
    print(f'=== Comprehensive Scan: {target} ===')
    print('Running: nmap -sV -sC -O -p 1-1024\n')
    
    # -sV: Version detection
    # -sC: Default scripts
    # -p 1-1024: First 1024 ports
    scanner.scan(target, '1-1024', arguments='-sV -sC')
    
    for host in scanner.all_hosts():
        print(f'Host: {host}')
        print(f'Hostname: {scanner[host].hostname()}')
        print(f'State: {scanner[host].state()}')
        
        for proto in scanner[host].all_protocols():
            print(f'\nProtocol: {proto}')
            print(f'{"PORT":>7s}  {"STATE":8s}  {"SERVICE":12s}  {"VERSION"}')
            print('-' * 60)
            
            ports = sorted(scanner[host][proto].keys())
            for port in ports:
                info = scanner[host][proto][port]
                state = info['state']
                name = info.get('name', '')
                product = info.get('product', '')
                version = info.get('version', '')
                extra = info.get('extrainfo', '')
                
                ver_str = f'{product} {version} {extra}'.strip()
                print(f'{port:5d}/{proto}  {state:8s}  {name:12s}  {ver_str}')
    
    # Export results
    print('\n--- Scan Info ---')
    print(f'Command: {scanner.command_line()}')
    print(f'Scan method: {scanner.scaninfo()}')
    
    return scanner

# Usage
results = comprehensive_scan('scanme.nmap.org')

complete_examples.title: Example 10: Network Mapper — Full Reconnaissance Report
complete_examples.description: Combine all techniques into a complete network reconnaissance tool.
complete_examples.code:
import socket
import concurrent.futures
import time
import json

class NetworkMapper:
    def __init__(self, target):
        self.target = target
        self.results = {
            'target': target,
            'scan_time': '',
            'hostname': '',
            'ip_address': '',
            'open_ports': [],
            'services': []
        }
    
    def resolve_target(self):
        """Resolve hostname to IP."""
        try:
            ip = socket.gethostbyname(self.target)
            self.results['ip_address'] = ip
            self.results['hostname'] = self.target
            print(f'Target: {self.target} -> {ip}')
            return ip
        except socket.gaierror:
            print(f'Cannot resolve {self.target}')
            return None
    
    def port_scan(self, ports=range(1, 1025), threads=50):
        """Threaded port scan."""
        open_ports = []
        lock = __import__('threading').Lock()
        
        def check(port):
            try:
                s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
                s.settimeout(1)
                if s.connect_ex((self.results['ip_address'], port)) == 0:
                    with lock:
                        open_ports.append(port)
                s.close()
            except:
                pass
        
        with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(max_workers=threads) as ex:
            ex.map(check, ports)
        
        self.results['open_ports'] = sorted(open_ports)
        print(f'Open ports: {self.results["open_ports"]}')
        return open_ports
    
    def service_detection(self):
        """Banner grab all open ports."""
        for port in self.results['open_ports']:
            service = {'port': port, 'banner': '', 'service_name': ''}
            try:
                s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
                s.settimeout(3)
                s.connect((self.results['ip_address'], port))
                try:
                    banner = s.recv(1024).decode('utf-8', errors='replace').strip()
                except socket.timeout:
                    s.send(b'HEAD / HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: test\r\n\r\n')
                    banner = s.recv(4096).decode('utf-8', errors='replace').strip()
                service['banner'] = banner[:200]
                try:
                    service['service_name'] = socket.getservbyport(port, 'tcp')
                except:
                    service['service_name'] = 'unknown'
                s.close()
            except:
                pass
            self.results['services'].append(service)
            print(f'  {port}/tcp -> {service["service_name"]}: {service["banner"][:50]}')
    
    def generate_report(self):
        """Generate JSON reconnaissance report."""
        self.results['scan_time'] = time.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')
        
        print('\n' + '=' * 60)
        print('RECONNAISSANCE REPORT')
        print('=' * 60)
        print(f'Target:    {self.results["hostname"]}')
        print(f'IP:        {self.results["ip_address"]}')
        print(f'Scan Time: {self.results["scan_time"]}')
        print(f'Open Ports: {len(self.results["open_ports"])}')
        print('-' * 60)
        for svc in self.results['services']:
            print(f'  {svc["port"]:5d}/tcp  {svc["service_name"]:10s}  {svc["banner"][:40]}')
        print('=' * 60)
        
        return json.dumps(self.results, indent=2)

# Full recon
mapper = NetworkMapper('scanme.nmap.org')
mapper.resolve_target()
mapper.port_scan(range(1, 1025))
mapper.service_detection()
report = mapper.generate_report()
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
This is a complete reconnaissance toolkit in one class:
1. resolve_target() maps hostname to IP
2. port_scan() finds open ports using 50 threads
3. service_detection() grabs banners from each open port
4. generate_report() creates a formatted JSON report
5. The JSON output can be saved to file for documentation
6. Professional pen testers create similar reports at the start of every engagement
7. This class is the foundation for building larger automated tools

complete_examples.title: Example 11: UDP Service Scanner
complete_examples.description: Scan critical UDP services — DNS, SNMP, NTP — which are often overlooked.
complete_examples.code:
import socket

# Service-specific probes for UDP
UDP_PROBES = {
    53: {   # DNS
        'name': 'DNS',
        'probe': bytes.fromhex(
            'aa aa 01 00 00 01 00 00 00 00 00 00'
            '07 76 65 72 73 69 6f 6e'
            '04 62 69 6e 64 00 00 10 00 03'
        ).replace(b' ', b''),
        'match': lambda d: b'version' in d.lower() or len(d) > 10
    },
    123: {  # NTP
        'name': 'NTP',
        'probe': b'\x1b' + b'\x00' * 47,  # NTP v3 client request
        'match': lambda d: len(d) >= 48
    },
    161: {  # SNMP
        'name': 'SNMP',
        'probe': bytes.fromhex(
            '302602010104067075626c6963'
            'a019020400020100020100'
            '300b300906052b060102010500'
        ),
        'match': lambda d: d[0:1] == b'\x30'  # ASN.1 sequence
    }
}

def udp_service_scan(target, ports=None):
    """Scan UDP ports with service-specific probes."""
    if ports is None:
        ports = UDP_PROBES.keys()
    
    print(f'UDP Service Scan: {target}')
    print('-' * 50)
    
    results = []
    for port in ports:
        probe_info = UDP_PROBES.get(port, {
            'name': 'Unknown',
            'probe': b'\x00',
            'match': lambda d: len(d) > 0
        })
        
        try:
            sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_DGRAM)
            sock.settimeout(3)
            sock.sendto(probe_info['probe'], (target, port))
            
            data, _ = sock.recvfrom(1024)
            if probe_info['match'](data):
                status = 'OPEN'
                results.append((port, probe_info['name'], status))
                print(f'  [{status}] {port}/udp  {probe_info["name"]}')
                print(f'         Response: {data[:30].hex()}')
        except socket.timeout:
            print(f'  [OPEN|FILTERED] {port}/udp  {probe_info["name"]}')
        except ConnectionRefusedError:
            print(f'  [CLOSED] {port}/udp  {probe_info["name"]}')
        except Exception as e:
            print(f'  [ERROR] {port}/udp  {e}')
        finally:
            sock.close()
    
    return results

# Scan common UDP services
udp_service_scan('8.8.8.8', [53])  # Google DNS
complete_examples.ai_explanation:
UDP scanning requires service-specific probes:
1. DNS probe: A version.bind query — DNS servers respond to this
2. NTP probe: A standard time request (0x1b = NTP v3 client mode)
3. SNMP probe: A GET request with 'public' community string
4. Each probe has a 'match' function to validate responses
5. Unlike TCP, UDP has no handshake — we must send meaningful data
6. Timeout = open|filtered (can't distinguish without response)
7. ConnectionRefused = closed (ICMP port unreachable received)
8. Many critical attacks target UDP services (DNS amplification, SNMP enumeration)

complete_examples.title: Example 12: Scan Results to CSV Report
complete_examples.description: Export scan results to CSV format for professional reporting and analysis.
complete_examples.code:
import csv
import socket
import time
import os

def scan_to_csv(target, ports, output_file='scan_report.csv'):
    """Scan and export results directly to CSV."""
    timestamp = time.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')
    
    # Resolve target
    try:
        ip = socket.gethostbyname(target)
    except:
        ip = target
    
    results = []
    print(f'Scanning {target} ({ip})...')
    
    for port in ports:
        sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        sock.settimeout(1)
        result = sock.connect_ex((ip, port))
        
        if result == 0:
            # Get service name
            try:
                service = socket.getservbyport(port, 'tcp')
            except:
                service = 'unknown'
            
            # Try banner grab
            try:
                banner = sock.recv(512).decode('utf-8', errors='replace').strip()
            except:
                banner = ''
            
            results.append({
                'timestamp': timestamp,
                'target': target,
                'ip': ip,
                'port': port,
                'protocol': 'tcp',
                'state': 'open',
                'service': service,
                'banner': banner[:100]
            })
            print(f'  [OPEN] {port}/tcp {service}')
        
        sock.close()
    
    # Write CSV
    if results:
        fieldnames = ['timestamp', 'target', 'ip', 'port', 'protocol',
                      'state', 'service', 'banner']
        with open(output_file, 'w', newline='') as f:
            writer = csv.DictWriter(f, fieldnames=fieldnames)
            writer.writeheader()
            writer.writerows(results)
        
        print(f'\nReport saved: {output_file}')
        print(f'Entries: {len(results)} open ports documented')
    else:
        print('\nNo open ports found.')
    
    return results

# Run scan and export
scan_to_csv('scanme.nmap.org', range(1, 101), 'nmap_scan.csv')

complete_examples.title: Example 14: Complete Recon Pipeline — From Discovery to Report
complete_examples.description: Full automated reconnaissance: host discovery, port scan, service detection, vulnerability check, and report generation.
complete_examples.code:
import socket
import concurrent.futures
import time
import json

class ReconPipeline:
    """Complete reconnaissance pipeline."""
    
    def __init__(self, target):
        self.target = target
        self.report = {
            'meta': {'target': target, 'start_time': '', 'end_time': ''},
            'dns': {},
            'ports': [],
            'services': [],
            'findings': [],
            'risk_summary': {'critical': 0, 'high': 0, 'medium': 0, 'low': 0, 'info': 0}
        }
    
    def phase1_dns(self):
        """Phase 1: DNS Resolution."""
        print('[Phase 1] DNS Resolution')
        try:
            ip = socket.gethostbyname(self.target)
            self.report['dns'] = {'ip': ip, 'hostname': self.target}
            print(f'  Resolved: {self.target} -> {ip}')
            # Check reverse
            try:
                rev = socket.gethostbyaddr(ip)[0]
                self.report['dns']['reverse'] = rev
            except:
                pass
        except:
            print(f'  FAILED to resolve {self.target}')
    
    def phase2_portscan(self, ports=range(1, 1025)):
        """Phase 2: Fast port scan."""
        print(f'[Phase 2] Port Scan ({len(list(ports))} ports)')
        ip = self.report['dns'].get('ip', self.target)
        open_ports = []
        lock = __import__('threading').Lock()
        
        def check(port):
            try:
                s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
                s.settimeout(1)
                if s.connect_ex((ip, port)) == 0:
                    with lock:
                        open_ports.append(port)
                s.close()
            except:
                pass
        
        with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(50) as ex:
            ex.map(check, ports)
        
        self.report['ports'] = sorted(open_ports)
        print(f'  Found {len(open_ports)} open: {sorted(open_ports)}')
    
    def phase3_services(self):
        """Phase 3: Service identification."""
        print('[Phase 3] Service Detection')
        ip = self.report['dns'].get('ip', self.target)
        
        for port in self.report['ports']:
            svc = {'port': port, 'name': '', 'banner': '', 'version': ''}
            try:
                svc['name'] = socket.getservbyport(port, 'tcp')
            except:
                svc['name'] = 'unknown'
            
            try:
                s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
                s.settimeout(3)
                s.connect((ip, port))
                try:
                    banner = s.recv(1024).decode('utf-8', errors='replace').strip()
                except socket.timeout:
                    if port in [80, 443, 8080]:
                        s.send(b'HEAD / HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n')
                        banner = s.recv(4096).decode('utf-8', errors='replace').strip()
                    else:
                        banner = ''
                svc['banner'] = banner[:200]
                s.close()
            except:
                pass
            
            self.report['services'].append(svc)
            print(f'  {port}/tcp {svc["name"]}: {svc["banner"][:50]}')
    
    def phase4_analysis(self):
        """Phase 4: Basic vulnerability analysis."""
        print('[Phase 4] Analysis')
        
        for svc in self.report['services']:
            banner = svc['banner'].lower()
            
            # Check for old versions
            if 'openssh' in banner and any(v in banner for v in ['5.', '6.']):
                finding = f'Outdated OpenSSH on port {svc["port"]} — update immediately'
                self.report['findings'].append({'severity': 'HIGH', 'finding': finding})
                self.report['risk_summary']['high'] += 1
            
            if 'apache/2.4.7' in banner or 'apache/2.2' in banner:
                finding = f'Outdated Apache on port {svc["port"]} — multiple CVEs'
                self.report['findings'].append({'severity': 'HIGH', 'finding': finding})
                self.report['risk_summary']['high'] += 1
            
            if 'vsftpd 2.3.4' in banner:
                finding = f'CRITICAL: vsftpd 2.3.4 backdoor on port {svc["port"]}!'
                self.report['findings'].append({'severity': 'CRITICAL', 'finding': finding})
                self.report['risk_summary']['critical'] += 1
            
            # Info findings
            if svc['banner']:
                finding = f'Service banner disclosed on port {svc["port"]}: {svc["name"]}'
                self.report['findings'].append({'severity': 'INFO', 'finding': finding})
                self.report['risk_summary']['info'] += 1
        
        for f in self.report['findings']:
            icon = {'CRITICAL': '!!!', 'HIGH': '!!', 'MEDIUM': '!', 'LOW': '.', 'INFO': 'i'}[f['severity']]
            print(f'  [{icon}] {f["severity"]}: {f["finding"]}')
    
    def run(self):
        """Execute full pipeline."""
        self.report['meta']['start_time'] = time.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')
        print(f'\n{"="*60}')
        print(f'SYNAPSE RECON PIPELINE: {self.target}')
        print(f'{"="*60}\n')
        
        self.phase1_dns()
        self.phase2_portscan()
        self.phase3_services()
        self.phase4_analysis()
        
        self.report['meta']['end_time'] = time.strftime('%Y-%m-%d %H:%M:%S')
        
        print(f'\n{"="*60}')
        print('RISK SUMMARY')
        for sev, count in self.report['risk_summary'].items():
            if count > 0:
                print(f'  {sev.upper()}: {count}')
        print(f'{"="*60}')
        
        return self.report

# Run full recon
pipeline = ReconPipeline('scanme.nmap.org')
results = pipeline.run()

visual_diagram.title: Network Scanning & Reconnaissance Workflow
visual_diagram.content: 🔍 NETWORK SCANNING & RECONNAISSANCE WORKFLOW ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ⚖️ PHASE 0: LEGAL AUTHORISATION │ ├── ✅ Written permission obtained? ├── ✅ Scope defined (IP ranges, ports)? ├── ✅ Testing window agreed? └── ❌ NO PERMISSION = STOP — THIS IS ILLEGAL │ ▼ 🌐 PHASE 1: PASSIVE RECONNAISSANCE │ ├── WHOIS Lookup → Registrar, nameservers, dates ├── DNS Records → A, AAAA, MX, NS, TXT, CNAME ├── Subdomain Enum → Brute-force + certificate transparency ├── Reverse DNS → IP → hostname mapping └── Certificate Lookup → crt.sh for all issued certs │ ├── 🔑 NO packets sent to target — 100% legal │ ▼ 📡 PHASE 2: HOST DISCOVERY │ ├── ICMP Ping Sweep → Send Echo Request to each IP │ ├── Reply received → HOST IS ALIVE │ ├── No reply → Could be filtered (firewall) │ └── TTL value hints at OS: 64=Linux, 128=Windows │ ├── ARP Sweep (local network only) │ ├── Send ARP "who-has?" for each IP │ ├── Reply → HOST IS ALIVE + MAC address │ └── Cannot be blocked by software firewalls! │ └── TCP Ping → SYN to common ports (80, 443) └── SYN-ACK back → HOST IS ALIVE │ ▼ 🚪 PHASE 3: PORT SCANNING │ ├── TCP Connect Scan (socket.connect) │ ├── Completes full 3-way handshake │ ├── ✅ Reliable, no special privileges needed │ └── ❌ Noisy — logged by target │ ├── SYN Stealth Scan (Scapy, requires root) │ ├── Sends SYN only, reads response │ ├── SYN-ACK → OPEN (send RST to close) │ ├── RST → CLOSED │ ├── No response → FILTERED │ ├── ✅ Stealthy — no full connection logged │ └── ❌ Needs root/admin privileges │ ├── UDP Scan (protocol-specific probes) │ ├── Send service-specific probe │ ├── Response → OPEN │ ├── ICMP Port Unreachable → CLOSED │ ├── No response → OPEN|FILTERED (ambiguous!) │ └── ❌ Slow and unreliable │ └── PORT STATES: ├── OPEN = Service accepting connections ├── CLOSED = No service, port reachable └── FILTERED = Firewall blocking probes │ ▼ 🔎 PHASE 4: SERVICE DETECTION │ ├── Banner Grabbing → Connect and read welcome message │ ├── SSH: "SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_8.9p1" │ ├── HTTP: "Server: Apache/2.4.52" │ ├── FTP: "220 vsftpd 3.0.5" │ └── SMTP: "220 mail.example.com ESMTP Postfix" │ ├── Version Detection → nmap -sV │ └── Sends protocol-specific probes │ └── OS Fingerprinting → nmap -O ├── Analyses TCP/IP stack behaviour ├── TTL, window size, IP ID sequence └── Matches against 6000+ OS fingerprints │ ▼ ⚠️ PHASE 5: VULNERABILITY ANALYSIS │ ├── Match service versions against CVE databases ├── Check for default credentials ├── Identify misconfigurations └── Prioritise: CRITICAL → HIGH → MEDIUM → LOW │ ▼ 📄 PHASE 6: REPORTING │ ├── Document all findings with evidence ├── CSV/JSON export for tools ├── Executive summary for management └── Technical details for remediation team ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 🛡️ REMEMBER — THE GOLDEN RULES: 1. ALWAYS get written permission before scanning 2. NEVER scan systems you don't own or aren't authorised to test 3. Start PASSIVE, then ACTIVE — gather first, probe second 4. Document EVERYTHING — timestamps, commands, results 5. Use LEGAL targets for learning: scanme.nmap.org, your own VMs 6. Slow scans are stealthier — speed vs detection trade-off 7. TCP tells you WHAT'S OPEN, banners tell you WHAT'S RUNNING 8. UDP services are often overlooked — don't skip them! 9. Version numbers are your key to finding CVEs 10. Professional recon = Passive + Active + Analysis + Report ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ PYTHON SCANNING TOOLKIT: socket TCP/UDP connections, DNS lookups (built-in) scapy Raw packet crafting (SYN scans, ICMP, ARP) nmap python-nmap wrapper for full nmap power dns dnspython for advanced DNS queries csv Export results for reporting (built-in)

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 8: Handle Socket Exceptions Gracefully
practice_exercises.description: Write a scanner that handles ALL possible socket errors.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's work on socket fundamentals! Write a scanner that handles ALL possible socket errors.
practice_exercises.starter_code:
import socket
import errno

def safe_scan(target, port):
    """Scan with comprehensive error handling."""
    try:
        sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        sock.settimeout(2)
        result = sock.connect_ex((target, port))
        
        if result == 0:
            return 'open'
        elif result == errno.ECONNREFUSED:  # 111
            return 'closed (connection refused)'
        elif result == errno.ETIMEDOUT:  # 110
            return 'filtered (timeout)'
        elif result == errno.EHOSTUNREACH:  # 113
            return 'host unreachable'
        else:
            return f'error (errno={result})'
    
    # TODO: Handle these exceptions
    except socket.timeout:
        return '_____'
    except socket.gaierror:
        return '_____'
    except PermissionError:
        return '_____'
    except OSError as e:
        return f'OS error: {e}'
    finally:
        try:
            sock.close()
        except:
            pass

# Test various scenarios
print(safe_scan('scanme.nmap.org', 80))
print(safe_scan('scanme.nmap.org', 12345))
print(safe_scan('nonexistent.invalid', 80))

closed (connection refused)
DNS resolution failed

hints.message: socket.timeout = connection took too long. socket.gaierror = DNS couldn't resolve the hostname.
PermissionError happens when scanning privileged ports without root. Always use finally to close the socket.
except socket.timeout: return 'filtered (socket timeout)'; except socket.gaierror: return 'DNS resolution failed'; except PermissionError: return 'permission denied'

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 9: Measure Network Latency
practice_exercises.description: Measure the round-trip time to a target on different ports.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Let's work on socket fundamentals! Measure the round-trip time to a target on different ports.
practice_exercises.starter_code:
import socket
import time

def measure_rtt(target, port, attempts=5):
    """Measure TCP round-trip time."""
    times = []
    
    for i in range(attempts):
        sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        sock.settimeout(5)
        
        start = time.time()
        result = sock.connect_ex((target, port))
        elapsed = (time.time() - start) * 1000  # Convert to ms
        
        if result == 0:
            times.append(elapsed)
        sock.close()
    
    if times:
        # TODO: Calculate min, max, average
        avg = sum(times) / len(times)
        print(f'  {target}:{port} — min={min(times):.1f}ms  '
              f'max={_____(times):.1f}ms  avg={_____:.1f}ms  '
              f'({len(times)}/{attempts} successful)')
    else:
        print(f'  {target}:{port} — all attempts failed')

print('Network Latency Test:')
measure_rtt('scanme.nmap.org', 80)
measure_rtt('scanme.nmap.org', 22)
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Network Latency Test:
  scanme.nmap.org:80 — min=85.2ms  max=92.1ms  avg=88.4ms  (5/5 successful)
  scanme.nmap.org:22 — min=84.9ms  max=91.8ms  avg=87.6ms  (5/5 successful)

hints.message: RTT (round-trip time) tells you how far away the target is. Lower = closer/faster.
Use min(), max(), and sum()/len() for statistics. Multiply by 1000 to convert seconds to milliseconds.
max(times) for maximum; avg for average (already calculated above)

practice_exercises.starter_code:
import socket
import time
import concurrent.futures

def sequential(target, ports):
    start = time.time()
    open_p = []
    for p in ports:
        s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        s.settimeout(1)
        if s.connect_ex((target, p)) == 0:
            open_p.append(p)
        s.close()
    return sorted(open_p), time.time() - start

def threaded(target, ports, workers=50):
    start = time.time()
    open_p = []
    lock = __import__('threading').Lock()
    def check(p):
        s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        s.settimeout(1)
        if s.connect_ex((target, p)) == 0:
            with lock: open_p.append(p)
        s.close()
    with concurrent.futures.ThreadPoolExecutor(workers) as ex:
        ex.map(check, ports)
    return sorted(open_p), time.time() - start

print(f'Threaded:   {len(thr_ports)} open, {thr_time:.2f}s, {200/thr_time:.0f} p/s')

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 18: Port Knocking Detector
practice_exercises.description: Detect if a service requires port knocking by testing access before and after a knock sequence.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Time for faster scanning! Detect if a service requires port knocking by testing access before and after a knock sequence.
practice_exercises.starter_code:
import socket
import time

def port_knock(target, knock_sequence, final_port):
    """Try port knocking: connect to sequence then check final port."""
    # Step 1: Check if final port is already open
    s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    s.settimeout(2)
    before = s.connect_ex((target, final_port)) == 0
    s.close()
    print(f'Before knock: port {final_port} is {"OPEN" if before else "CLOSED"}')
    
    # Step 2: Send the knock sequence
    print(f'Knocking: {knock_sequence}')
    for port in knock_sequence:
        s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
        s.settimeout(0.5)
        s.connect_ex((target, port))  # Just try to connect
        s.close()
        # TODO: Small delay between knocks
        time.sleep(_____)
    
    # Step 3: Check final port again
    time.sleep(0.5)  # Wait for firewall rule to activate
    s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    s.settimeout(2)
    after = s.connect_ex((target, final_port)) == 0
    s.close()
    print(f'After knock:  port {final_port} is {"OPEN" if after else "CLOSED"}')
    
    if not before and after:
        print(f'Port knocking WORKS! Sequence: {knock_sequence}')
    elif before:
        print('Port was already open (no knocking needed)')
    else:
        print('Port still closed (wrong sequence or no knocking configured)')

# Example: knock on 7000,8000,9000 then check SSH
port_knock('scanme.nmap.org', [7000, 8000, 9000], 22)
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Before knock: port 22 is OPEN
Knocking: [7000, 8000, 9000]
After knock:  port 22 is OPEN
Port was already open (no knocking needed)

hints.message: Port knocking is a security technique where a sequence of connections to specific ports opens a hidden service.
Each knock needs a small delay (0.1-0.5 seconds) for the firewall to register it.
time.sleep(0.2) for a 200ms delay between knocks

import socket

def http_headers(target, port=80):
    sock = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    sock.settimeout(5)
    sock.connect((target, port))
    
    # TODO: Send HTTP HEAD request
    request = f'HEAD / HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: {target}\r\n\r\n'
    sock.send(request._____())
    
    response = sock.recv(4096).decode('utf-8', errors='replace')
    sock.close()
    
    print('Response Headers:')
    for line in response.split('\r\n'):
        if line:
            print(f'  {line}')
    
    # Extract specific security-relevant headers
    print('\nSecurity Analysis:')
    headers_lower = response.lower()
    checks = [
        ('server:', 'Server version disclosed'),
        ('x-powered-by:', 'Technology stack disclosed'),
        ('strict-transport-security:', 'HSTS enabled'),
        ('x-frame-options:', 'Clickjacking protection'),
        ('content-security-policy:', 'CSP enabled'),
    ]
    for header, meaning in checks:
        found = header in headers_lower
        icon = '✅' if ('strict' in header or 'x-frame' in header or 'content-security' in header) and found else '⚠️' if found else '❌'
        status = 'FOUND' if found else 'MISSING'
        print(f'  {icon} {meaning}: {status}')

import socket

PROTOCOL_PROBES = {
    'ssh': {'send': None, 'port': 22},
    'http': {'send': b'HEAD / HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n', 'port': 80},
    'ftp': {'send': None, 'port': 21},
    'smtp': {'send': b'EHLO scanner\r\n', 'port': 25},
}

def multi_banner(target):
    for proto, config in PROTOCOL_PROBES.items():
        port = config['port']
        try:
            s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
            s.settimeout(3)
            s.connect((target, port))
            
            if config['send'] is None:
                # TODO: Just receive (auto-banner)
                data = s.recv(1024)
            else:
                # TODO: Send probe then receive
                s.send(config['_____'])
                data = s.recv(4096)
            
            banner = data.decode('utf-8', errors='replace').strip()[:80]
            print(f'  [{proto:5s}] Port {port}: {banner}')
            s.close()
        except Exception as e:
            print(f'  [{proto:5s}] Port {port}: {e}')

  [smtp ] Port 25: [Errno 111] Connection refused

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 26: Automated Service Fingerprint
practice_exercises.description: Identify services by sending protocol-specific probes and matching responses.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Service detection time! Identify services by sending protocol-specific probes and matching responses.
practice_exercises.starter_code:
import socket
import re

FINGERPRINTS = [
    {'name': 'SSH', 'match': r'^SSH-\d\.\d', 'probe': None},
    {'name': 'HTTP', 'match': r'^HTTP/\d\.\d', 'probe': b'GET / HTTP/1.0\r\n\r\n'},
    {'name': 'FTP', 'match': r'^220[- ]', 'probe': None},
    {'name': 'SMTP', 'match': r'^220[- ].*SMTP', 'probe': None},
    {'name': 'MySQL', 'match': r'^.\x00\x00\x00\x0a', 'probe': None},
    {'name': 'Redis', 'match': r'^-ERR|^\+', 'probe': b'PING\r\n'},
]

def identify_service(target, port):
    for fp in FINGERPRINTS:
        try:
            s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
            s.settimeout(3)
            s.connect((target, port))
            
            if fp['probe']:
                s.send(fp['probe'])
            
            data = s.recv(1024).decode('utf-8', errors='replace')
            s.close()
            
            # TODO: Match response against fingerprint regex
            if re.search(fp['_____'], data):
                return fp['name'], data.strip()[:60]
        except:
            pass
    
    return 'Unknown', ''

# Test
for port in [22, 80]:
    service, banner = identify_service('scanme.nmap.org', port)
    print(f'Port {port}: {service} — {banner}')
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Port 22: SSH — SSH-2.0-OpenSSH_6.6.1p1 Ubuntu-2ubuntu2.13
Port 80: HTTP — HTTP/1.1 200 OK

hints.message: Each fingerprint has a regex pattern. Try each probe until one matches the response.
re.search(pattern, data) returns a match if the pattern appears anywhere in the response.
fp['match'] is the regex pattern to search for

practice_exercises.title: Exercise 27: Detect Web Technologies
practice_exercises.description: Identify web server technologies from HTTP response headers and body.
practice_exercises.ai_intro: Service detection time! Identify web server technologies from HTTP response headers and body.
practice_exercises.starter_code:
import socket

def detect_web_tech(target, port=80):
    s = socket.socket(socket.AF_INET, socket.SOCK_STREAM)
    s.settimeout(5)
    s.connect((target, port))
    
    req = f'GET / HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: {target}\r\n\r\n'
    s.send(req.encode())
    resp = s.recv(8192).decode('utf-8', errors='replace')
    s.close()
    
    techs = []
    resp_lower = resp.lower()
    
    # Header checks
    tech_headers = {
        'x-powered-by: php': 'PHP',
        'x-powered-by: asp.net': 'ASP.NET',
        'server: apache': 'Apache',
        'server: nginx': 'Nginx',
        'server: iis': 'Microsoft IIS',
        'x-drupal': 'Drupal CMS',
    }
    
    for header, tech in tech_headers.items():
        if header in resp_lower:
            techs.append(tech)
    
    # Body checks
    body_checks = {
        'wp-content': 'WordPress',
        'joomla': 'Joomla CMS',
        'drupal.js': 'Drupal CMS',
        'react': 'React.js',
        'angular': 'Angular',
    }
    
    for marker, tech in body_checks.items():
        if marker in resp_lower and tech not in techs:
            techs.append(tech)
    
    print(f'Technologies detected on {target}:')
    for t in techs:
        print(f'  ✓ {t}')
    if not techs:
        print('  No technologies detected')

detect_web_tech('scanme.nmap.org')
practice_exercises.expected_output:
Technologies detected on scanme.nmap.org:
  ✓ Apache

hints.message: HTTP headers and page content reveal what technologies a site uses.
Server header shows the web server. X-Powered-By reveals the backend language. Body content reveals CMS.
Match known technology markers in both headers and body content
